natural_occurrence.description: Calcium oxide does not occur naturally in free form (it reacts with moisture). It is produced by heating limestone (calcium carbonate) in kilns.

manufacturing.description: Produced by heating limestone (calcium carbonate, CaCO3) to very high temperatures (above 825°C) in a lime kiln. This drives off carbon dioxide, leaving calcium oxide. When water is added, it becomes calcium hydroxide (slaked lime).

other_uses.industrial: Steel manufacturing, cement production, glass manufacturing, paper production, water treatment.
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Rarely used directly; converted to calcium hydroxide for pharmaceutical use.
other_uses.household: Historical use in lighting (limelight), hot packs (exothermic reaction with water).

eu.conditions: Used as processing aid; typically hydrated to calcium hydroxide upon contact with moisture in food.

usa.notes: Limited use; typically converts to calcium hydroxide in food applications.

safety_evaluations.jecfa_adi: not specified (acceptable for use, converts to calcium hydroxide in food)

commonly_found_in: Corn processing (typically hydrated to calcium hydroxide first)
fun_facts: Called 'QUICKLIME' because it reacts quickly and vigorously with water, generating intense heat
The reaction with water is so exothermic it can boil the water - never add water directly to quicklime!
Used historically for 'limelight' in theaters - heated quicklime glows with brilliant white light
Almost never used directly in food - immediately converts to calcium hydroxide (slaked lime) when it contacts moisture
Made by humans for over 5,000 years - one of the oldest chemical processes known
The same process and product used to make cement and mortar for construction
Extremely caustic and reactive - much less safe to handle than its hydrated form (calcium hydroxide)
